summary: The post argues that the American higher education system has become a costly arms race where students continually escalate spending on degrees to improve their relative ranking, but like neighbors competing to have the nicest cars, no one actually gains lasting advantage. O'Neil draws on Tom Slee's book to explain how arms races require collective agreements to resolve, but the education system's many stakeholders—employers who benefit from over-credentialed candidates, colleges that love administrative growth, and students rationally pursuing rankings—all have incentives to continue the escalation. She criticizes a Third Way proposal to reduce tuition through administrative cuts as unrealistic while dismissing minimum wage increases, calling this prioritization backwards. Her suggested solution is widespread free or near-free basic education, though she acknowledges the political difficulty of achieving any collective agreement.
key_points: Education functions as an arms race where escalating costs don't change relative rankings, just like neighbors competing to own the nicest cars
All major stakeholders benefit from the current dysfunctional system: employers get overqualified workers, colleges expand administration, and individual students rationally pursue higher rankings
Federal loan programs combined with non-dischargeable student debt have created a feedback loop driving up tuition and student debt
Proposed 'solutions' like pegging government aid to graduation rates would create perverse incentives without addressing the underlying arms race dynamics
Raising the minimum wage would more directly address income inequality than trying to push more people through an expensive credentialing system
money_quotes: From the point of view of employers, rankings are great and they can be sure to pick the highest ranked individuals from that system, even if that means – as it often does – having Ph.D. graduates working in mailrooms.
In other words, it's a shitshow, and possibly the only real disruption that could improve it would be widespread and universally respected basic and free-ish education.
From the point of view of the colleges, they're getting to hire more and more administrators, which means growth, which they love.
Am I the only one who thinks raising the minimum wage would help more to address income inequality and is easier to imagine working?
So don't expect any help from them to add sanity to this system.
key_insight: Higher education has become a collective action problem where everyone rationally escalates their investment in credentials, but because it's a zero-sum ranking system, the only winners are the institutions extracting money from the arms race.
